name: Platyrrhini
taxonomicNote: Platyrrhini parvorder — New World monkeys of Central and South America. Treated here as a single family-level group spanning Cebidae, Callitrichidae, Atelidae, and Pitheciidae for consistency with the portal's presentation.

children.name: Cebus
children.description: Gracile capuchins are small to medium-sized monkeys with a reputation as the most intelligent New World primates, capable of tool use — cracking nuts with stones — and complex social manipulation. Widespread across Central and South American forests.

children.name: Cebus capucinus
children.description: The white-faced capuchin is one of the most studied New World primates and a keystone species in Central American forests. It exhibits remarkable tool use, cultural traditions, and coalition politics within troops of 6–20 individuals. Populations in Panama use stone tools to process food and show regional behavioural dialects.

children.name: Cebus olivaceus
children.description: The wedge-capped capuchin inhabits the llanos and gallery forests of Venezuela and Guyana, living in groups of up to 30 individuals led by a dominant male. It is named for the dark wedge-shaped cap of fur on its crown that contrasts sharply with its pale face. Like other Cebus, it forages opportunistically for fruit, invertebrates, and small vertebrates.

children.name: Cebus albifrons
children.description: One of the most taxonomically complex capuchins, the white-fronted capuchin ranges across much of the western Amazon basin and has numerous subspecies that may represent distinct species. It inhabits a wide variety of forest types from terra firme to várzea and montane forest up to 2,000 m. Groups of 6–25 animals forage widely for fruit, seeds, and invertebrates.

children.name: Cebus kaapori
children.description: The Ka'apor capuchin is a Critically Endangered gracile capuchin restricted to forest remnants in eastern Pará and western Maranhão, Brazil, where it is named after the indigenous Ka'apor people. It was only scientifically described in 1992 and is one of the rarest capuchins, threatened by severe deforestation. Its small range and low population density make it highly vulnerable to habitat loss and hunting.

children.name: Cebus imitator
children.description: Recently split from Cebus capucinus, the Panama capuchin is endemic to Panama and Costa Rica and is the subject of long-term field studies on Barro Colorado Island in Panama. These studies have revealed extraordinary social complexity including social bonds, reconciliation behaviour, and systematic stone tool use for nut cracking. It is listed as Least Concern but depends on intact forest corridors.

children.name: Sapajus
children.description: Robust capuchins of South America, recently split from Cebus, are stout-bodied monkeys known for their exceptional tool use including the systematic cracking of hard palm nuts with heavy stones — behaviour passed culturally between generations.

children.name: Sapajus apella
children.description: The tufted capuchin is the most widespread robust capuchin, ranging across most of Amazonia and adjacent forests. Males bear a distinctive black cap of stiffened hair that projects forward, giving the genus its common name. Wild populations in Piauí, Brazil, have been documented cracking Sclerolobium nuts with quartzite anvils — a behaviour confirmed archaeologically to be at least 3,000 years old.

children.name: Sapajus libidinosus
children.description: The black-striped capuchin inhabits the Caatinga, Cerrado, and transitional forests of northeastern and central Brazil and is one of the most-studied tool-using primates outside the great apes. Populations habitually use heavy quartzite stones as hammers and flat rocks as anvils to process encased food including cacti, seeds, and shellfish. Its tool traditions are transmitted socially between generations and vary between populations.

children.name: Sapajus nigritus
children.description: The black capuchin is largely restricted to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil and adjacent Argentina, where it is an important seed disperser in this critically threatened biome. Adults are predominantly blackish-brown with a pale face, and males can weigh up to 4 kg. It is threatened by habitat fragmentation and hunting, and is listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN.

children.name: Sapajus robustus
children.description: The crested capuchin is an Endangered Atlantic Forest endemic, restricted to forests in Bahia and Minas Gerais, Brazil, where it occurs at low densities in degraded fragments. It is closely related to Sapajus nigritus and the two form a superspecies complex. Deforestation for agriculture has reduced its range to less than 10% of its original extent.

children.name: Sapajus xanthosternos
children.description: The yellow-breasted capuchin is a Critically Endangered Atlantic Forest primate limited to a small area of Bahia, Brazil, bearing a striking yellow-orange chest that distinguishes it from other robust capuchins. Fewer than 180 individuals were estimated to remain in the wild as of the early 2000s, and the species is one of the 25 most endangered primates globally. Habitat destruction and hunting are the primary threats.

children.name: Sapajus flavius
children.description: The blond capuchin was rediscovered in 2006 in the Atlantic Forest of northeastern Brazil after being unknown to science since the early 1800s. Its pale golden pelage distinguishes it immediately from other capuchins. It is listed as Critically Endangered due to the extreme fragmentation of its Atlantic Forest habitat in Pernambuco and Paraíba.

children.name: Sapajus cay
children.description: The hooded capuchin inhabits gallery forests, cerrado, and riparian woodlands of the Gran Chaco and adjacent regions of Paraguay, Bolivia, and Brazil. It is notable for its flexibility, thriving in dry, seasonally flooded, and fragmented habitats where other capuchins would not survive. Tool use has been documented in some populations, particularly the use of sticks to probe for insects.

children.name: Sapajus macrocephalus
children.description: The large-headed capuchin is a western Amazonian robust capuchin, ranging across Peru, Ecuador, and adjacent Brazil and Bolivia, in terra firme and flooded forests. It was elevated to full species status following revisions of Sapajus apella. It lives in groups of 8–15 and occupies the mid-to-upper forest canopy where it forages for fruit and invertebrates.

children.name: Saimiri
children.description: Squirrel monkeys are small, hyperactive primates of Amazonian and Central American forests, living in large, noisy troops of up to 500 individuals. Despite their small size, they have the largest brain-to-body ratio of any primate.

children.name: Saimiri sciureus
children.description: The common squirrel monkey is the most frequently seen squirrel monkey across Amazonian Brazil, Colombia, and the Guianas, forming troops of up to 500 animals that move through the forest canopy in a nearly constant chorus of chirps and peeps. Its bright yellow-green back and distinctive black-and-white face are unmistakeable. In many areas it is partially sympatric with tufted capuchins and will join mixed-species troops to exploit food sources.

children.name: Saimiri boliviensis
children.description: The Bolivian squirrel monkey inhabits the southwestern Amazon basin in Bolivia, Peru, and adjacent Brazil, where it occupies humid lowland forests and forest edges. It is one of the most widely used primates in biomedical research. During the breeding season males undergo pronounced 'fatting' — a dramatic increase in body mass to compete during the mating season.

children.name: Saimiri oerstedii
children.description: The Central American squirrel monkey is restricted to a narrow strip of Pacific coastal forest in Costa Rica and Panama and is one of the most endangered primates in Central America. It was named for Danish botanist Anders Sandøe Ørsted who collected specimens in Costa Rica in the 1840s. Conservation efforts including corridor planting and power-line modifications have helped stabilise some populations.

children.name: Saimiri ustus
children.description: The bare-eared squirrel monkey is endemic to the central Amazon basin of Brazil, named for its lack of the ear tufts present in other squirrel monkeys. It inhabits terra firme forests between the Madeira and Tapajós rivers in a restricted range. Little studied in the wild, it appears to live in typical large squirrel monkey troops and favours high-canopy fruit-rich forest.

children.name: Saimiri vanzolinii
children.description: The black-crowned squirrel monkey is the rarest squirrel monkey, restricted to the varzea (seasonally flooded) forests of the Japurá and Juruá river confluence in the Brazilian Amazon. Named after Brazilian herpetologist Paulo Vanzolini, it is listed as Vulnerable due to its extremely small range. It is specialised for flooded forest habitat and shows adaptations for navigating the inundated understorey.

children.name: Saimiri cassiquiarensis
children.description: Humboldt's squirrel monkey was described from specimens collected near the Casiquiare canal in Venezuela and ranges across Venezuela, eastern Colombia, and northwestern Brazil. It occupies gallery forests and flooded lowland habitats along major river systems. Like all squirrel monkeys, it forms large mixed-sex troops and relies heavily on insects to supplement a fruit-dominated diet.

children.name: Alouatta
children.description: Howler monkeys are the loudest land animals on Earth — their roars, produced by an enlarged hyoid bone, carry up to 5 km through dense forest and serve as long-distance territorial announcements. They are the most widely distributed New World monkeys.

children.name: Alouatta seniculus
children.description: The Venezuelan red howler is the most widespread howler monkey, ranging from Venezuela and the Guianas across much of Amazonian South America. Adult males are reddish-brown with a massive hyoid bone that amplifies their ear-splitting territorial calls heard at dawn and dusk. It is a keystone species in many Neotropical forests, dispersing large seeds of trees that depend on large-bodied frugivores.

children.name: Alouatta palliata
children.description: The mantled howler ranges from Mexico through Central America to northwestern South America and is the most studied howler, with long-term datasets from Barro Colorado Island in Panama exceeding 90 years. Its 'mantle' of long golden-brown flank hairs gives it its common name. Unlike most primates, it can subsist on a highly folivorous diet, fermenting leaves in an enlarged caecum.

children.name: Alouatta caraya
children.description: The black-and-gold howler is one of the most sexually dichromatic of all primates: males are jet-black while females and young are yellow-olive. It occupies the Gran Chaco, cerrado, and gallery forests of central South America — Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, and Paraguay. It tolerates more seasonal and fragmented habitats than most howlers, making it relatively resilient to deforestation.

children.name: Alouatta pigra
children.description: The Mexican black howler is restricted to the Yucatán Peninsula region of Mexico, Belize, and Guatemala, where it is listed as Endangered due to rapid deforestation. Both sexes are uniformly glossy black, unlike the sexually dichromatic congeners. It is an important ecotourism draw in sites such as the Cockscomb Basin Wildlife Sanctuary in Belize.

children.name: Alouatta guariba
children.description: The brown howler is endemic to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil and northeastern Argentina, one of the world's most threatened biomes. Males are reddish-chestnut to dark brown with conspicuous beards, while females are yellowish-olive. It has suffered mass mortality from yellow fever outbreaks in the Atlantic Forest, compounding the pressure of deforestation on remaining populations.

children.name: Alouatta belzebul
children.description: The red-handed howler is a distinctive Amazonian species with red or orange hands and feet that contrast sharply with its otherwise black pelage. It inhabits forests of Pará and Maranhão in northeastern Brazil and is threatened by rampant deforestation in one of Brazil's most heavily cleared regions. Its loud dawn chorus is a reliable indicator of intact forest patches in otherwise degraded landscapes.

children.name: Alouatta nigerrima
children.description: The Amazon black howler is a small, poorly known howler of the eastern Amazon basin, restricted to an area between the Xingu and Tocantins rivers in Pará, Brazil. Both sexes are uniformly black, and it is closely related to Alouatta belzebul. It is considered Vulnerable due to deforestation and fragmentation of its restricted Amazonian range.

children.name: Alouatta discolor
children.description: Spix's red-handed howler was long treated as a subspecies of Alouatta belzebul before being elevated to full species status. It inhabits forests of central Amazonia between the Tapajós and Madeira rivers and shows subtle pelage differences from its eastern relative. Like other howlers it is a leaf-eating specialist that can survive in isolated forest fragments if enough tall trees remain.

children.name: Alouatta sara
children.description: The Bolivian red howler is restricted to Bolivia and adjacent Peru, where it inhabits lowland and foothill forests of the Amazon basin. It was elevated from subspecies of Alouatta seniculus status and is distinguished by molecular and morphological characters. It occupies a range that includes parts of Madidi and Amboró National Parks and is considered relatively secure in protected areas.

children.name: Alouatta puruensis
children.description: The Purus red howler was described as a species of the Seniculus group, inhabiting forests along the Purus and Juruá river systems in the Brazilian Amazon. Its taxonomy and limits remain debated but it is now generally accepted as a valid species. Ranging in forests that are increasingly threatened by agriculture and logging along major Amazonian waterways.

children.name: Alouatta macconnelli
children.description: The Guyanan red howler inhabits the Guiana Shield forests of Venezuela, Guyana, Suriname, and French Guiana, as well as Trinidadian forests where it was introduced. Named for British naturalist Frederick McConnell, it is a robust red howler with orange-red fur in males. It is listed as Least Concern and is common in remote forests of the Guiana Shield.

children.name: Alouatta coibensis
children.description: The Coiba Island howler is an island endemic of Panama, restricted to Coiba Island and the Azuero Peninsula — one of the smallest ranges of any howler monkey. Coiba was a penal colony for most of the twentieth century, which inadvertently preserved its forests and made it a national park. The population on Coiba Island has benefited from strict protection since the island became a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

children.name: Ateles
children.description: Spider monkeys are among the most acrobatic of all primates, using their prehensile tail as a fifth limb to swing through the forest canopy. They have the longest arms relative to body size of any New World monkey and are important seed dispersers in Neotropical forests.

children.name: Ateles geoffroyi
children.description: Geoffroy's spider monkey ranges from Mexico through Central America and into Colombia, making it the most northerly distributed spider monkey. Named for the great French naturalist Étienne Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire, it is Endangered due to severe forest loss across much of Central America. It is a highly frugivorous species whose large-seeded dispersal services are critical to forest regeneration.

children.name: Ateles paniscus
children.description: The Guiana spider monkey, jet black throughout, ranges across the Guiana Shield in Venezuela, the Guianas, and northern Brazil, preferring undisturbed high-canopy terra firme forest. It is considered a flagship species for Guiana Shield conservation. Its fission-fusion social system — where groups of 20–30 split into smaller foraging parties during the day — is similar to chimpanzees.

children.name: Ateles belzebuth
children.description: The white-bellied spider monkey ranges across the upper Amazon basin in Ecuador, Peru, Colombia, Venezuela, and Brazil, distinguished by its pale belly contrasting with dark limbs and back. It is Endangered due to hunting and deforestation. Large body size makes it a preferred target for bushmeat hunters, and it is among the first species to disappear from hunted forests.

children.name: Ateles chamek
children.description: The black-faced black spider monkey inhabits forests of Peru, Bolivia, and southwestern Brazil and is entirely black including the face, distinguishing it from sympatric spider monkeys with pale or flesh-coloured faces. It is Endangered and heavily hunted for bushmeat across its range. Long-term studies in Manu National Park have documented its remarkably varied fruit diet and role in regenerating disturbed forest.

children.name: Ateles hybridus
children.description: The brown spider monkey is restricted to forests in northern Colombia and northwestern Venezuela and is listed as Critically Endangered — one of the 25 most threatened primates in the world. Extremely high rates of deforestation in its range for cattle ranching and agriculture have reduced its numbers to possibly fewer than a few thousand individuals. Captive breeding and community-based conservation are underway in Colombia.

children.name: Ateles marginatus
children.description: The white-whiskered spider monkey is endemic to a restricted area south of the Amazon between the Tapajós and Xingu rivers in Pará, Brazil. Its black face is rimmed by white whiskers — a striking pattern that makes it unmistakeable in its range. It is Endangered by extensive deforestation associated with the BR-163 highway corridor and soy agriculture expansion.

children.name: Ateles fusciceps
children.description: The brown-headed spider monkey is restricted to the forests of Ecuador and adjacent Colombia and Panama, where it is Critically Endangered due to severe deforestation. Ecuador has lost over 90% of its Pacific coastal forest, pushing this species to the brink. It is one of the largest-bodied Neotropical primates and requires extensive continuous forest to maintain viable populations.

children.name: Lagothrix
children.description: Woolly monkeys are large, heavy-bodied primates of Amazonian and Andean foothill forests, named for their dense, soft fur. They are among the most frugivorous of all primates and critical seed dispersers of large-seeded trees in western Amazonia.

children.name: Lagothrix lagothricha
children.description: Humboldt's woolly monkey is the most widespread woolly monkey, ranging from Colombia and Venezuela south through the upper Amazon basin to Bolivia. Named for the great explorer Alexander von Humboldt, it is the classic 'lagotricha' of natural history collections. It is Vulnerable due to hunting and deforestation, and is highly prized as bushmeat — females with infants are often targeted, removing future reproductive investment.

children.name: Lagothrix cana
children.description: Geoffroy's woolly monkey, named for French naturalist Étienne Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire, inhabits forests of the central and upper Amazon in Brazil and Peru. It is Endangered, primarily due to hunting pressure, and populations have declined severely even in remote areas. Its grey-brown dense fur and prehensile tail make it a charismatic flagship for Amazonian conservation.

children.name: Lagothrix poeppigii
children.description: The silvery woolly monkey, named for German naturalist Eduard Poeppig, is found in the upper Amazon basin of Peru and Ecuador. It has a distinctive silvery-grey coat that makes it stand out among the brown-toned congeners. It is Endangered and is particularly sensitive to hunting pressure because of its slow reproductive rate and complex social bonds.

children.name: Lagothrix lugens
children.description: The Colombian woolly monkey is restricted to forests of the Andean foothills and lowland Amazon of Colombia, listed as Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List. It is one of the most threatened primates in South America due to decades of deforestation and persistent hunting across its already limited range. Conservation efforts focus on community reserves in the Colombian Amazon and Putumayo departments.

children.name: Lagothrix tschudii
children.description: The Peruvian woolly monkey, named for Swiss naturalist Johann Jakob von Tschudi, is restricted to the Peruvian and Bolivian Amazon. It inhabits lowland tropical forest and is Endangered due to hunting and habitat loss. Recent molecular analysis confirmed it as a species distinct from the widespread Lagothrix lagothricha.

children.name: Brachyteles
children.description: Muriquis, or woolly spider monkeys, are the largest New World primates and are endemic to the Atlantic Forest of Brazil. They are almost exclusively leaf-eating and are remarkable for their 'peaceful' egalitarian social system in which males rarely fight and females have high social autonomy.

children.name: Brachyteles arachnoides
children.description: The southern muriqui is restricted to small forest fragments in São Paulo and Paraná, Brazil, and is Endangered. It can weigh up to 15 kg, making it the largest New World primate. Long-term studies at the Fazenda Barreiro Rico have documented their gentle, egalitarian social life and the importance of large forest patches for maintaining viable groups.

children.name: Brachyteles hypoxanthus
children.description: The northern muriqui is Critically Endangered and exists in fewer than 25 fragmented populations across Minas Gerais, Espírito Santo, and Bahia, Brazil, with a total population estimated at around 2,000 individuals. Carlos Botelho State Park and RPPN Feliciano Miguel Abdala are critical refugia. It features prominently in all 25 Most Endangered Primates lists published since its recognition as a separate species.

children.name: Oreonax
children.description: A monotypic genus erected for the yellow-tailed woolly monkey of the Peruvian Andes, separated from Lagothrix on molecular grounds. It is among the rarest and most endangered of all primates.

children.name: Oreonax flavicauda
children.description: The yellow-tailed woolly monkey is Critically Endangered and restricted to cloud forests of the Peruvian Andes between 1,500 and 2,700 m in the departments of San Martín, Amazonas, La Libertad, Huánuco, and Loreto. Once thought extinct, it was rediscovered in 1974. Its distinctive yellow underside to the tail tip gives the species its common and scientific names, and its restricted high-altitude forest habitat is under sustained pressure from agriculture and logging.

children.name: Callithrix
children.description: Atlantic Forest marmosets are tiny, squirrel-sized primates with distinctive ear tufts and claws adapted for clinging to tree bark to gouge holes and feed on gum. They reliably give birth to non-identical twins and practise extensive cooperative infant care.

children.name: Callithrix jacchus
children.description: The common marmoset is the most widespread and familiar marmoset, native to northeastern Brazil but widely introduced to other parts of the country through the pet trade. It has become a key model organism in neuroscience research due to its small size, fast reproductive rate, and amenability to genetic manipulation. Troops of 3–15 cooperatively raise offspring, with older siblings acting as allomothers.

children.name: Callithrix penicillata
children.description: The black-tufted marmoset is native to the cerrado of central Brazil and is characterised by prominent black ear tufts contrasting with a white forehead blaze. It has colonised urban parks and gardens in Brasília and São Paulo following introductions and is now abundant in degraded habitats throughout central Brazil. It hybridises readily with the common marmoset in contact zones.

children.name: Callithrix geoffroyi
children.description: Geoffroy's tufted-ear marmoset is endemic to the Atlantic Forest of Espírito Santo and eastern Minas Gerais, Brazil, named for the renowned French naturalist Étienne Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire. It is distinguished by its white forehead, cheeks, and ear tufts against a dark crown. Listed as Vulnerable, it has lost much of its Atlantic Forest habitat and hybridises with introduced common marmosets, threatening its genetic integrity.

children.name: Callithrix aurita
children.description: The buffy-tufted marmoset is one of the most endangered of the Atlantic Forest marmosets, restricted to a small area of São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, and Minas Gerais. It is listed as Critically Endangered and is threatened by hybridisation with introduced common marmosets, in addition to habitat loss. It has pale buffy or whitish ear tufts and lacks the bold markings of its congeners.

children.name: Callithrix flaviceps
children.description: The buffy-headed marmoset is an Endangered Atlantic Forest endemic restricted to Espírito Santo and adjacent Minas Gerais in Brazil, where it inhabits forest at higher elevations than other marmosets. It is identified by its buffy-yellow head and small pale ear tufts. Its population is severely fragmented and threatened by both deforestation and hybridisation pressure from introduced Callithrix jacchus.

children.name: Callithrix kuhlii
children.description: Wied's marmoset is named for the zoologist Heinrich Kuhl and is endemic to the southern Bahia Atlantic Forest, one of the most biodiverse and threatened regions on Earth. It inhabits forest fragments and secondary vegetation in the cocoa-growing region of Bahia, where the traditional 'cabruca' shade-grown cocoa agroforestry system provides critical habitat. Listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List.

children.name: Cebuella
children.description: Pygmy marmosets are the world's smallest monkeys and among the smallest primates, weighing as little as 100 g. They are highly specialised gummivores, excavating tree bark with their distinctive procumbent lower incisors to create sap-wells that form the core of their small home ranges.

children.name: Cebuella pygmaea
children.description: The western pygmy marmoset inhabits the western Amazon basin in Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, and Brazil west of the Rio Juruá. It is the smallest monkey in the world, adults weighing about 100–150 g and measuring just 12–15 cm in body length. Family groups of 2–9 defend a set of preferred sap-wells and communicate through ultrasonic calls inaudible to humans.

children.name: Cebuella niveiventris
children.description: The eastern pygmy marmoset was recently split from Cebuella pygmaea on the basis of molecular and morphological differences; it inhabits the eastern Amazon from the Rio Juruá to the central Amazon in Brazil. Its white belly contrasts slightly more sharply with its barred brown-grey back compared to the western species. Both species are considered Least Concern but face threats from deforestation and the illegal pet trade.

children.name: Mico
children.description: Amazonian marmosets, recently split from Callithrix, are a diverse group of small primates endemic to the Brazilian Amazon, many of which have only been described in recent decades as their remote habitats have become accessible.

children.name: Mico argentatus
children.description: The silvery marmoset is one of the most distinctive Amazonian marmosets, its body almost entirely covered in white or pale silvery fur with a bare pinkish face and red-tinged ears. It is endemic to the eastern Amazon between the Tapajós and Tocantins rivers in Pará, Brazil. Despite its striking appearance, it was not described until 1766 by Linnaeus from a specimen originating in Suriname.

children.name: Mico melanurus
children.description: The black-tailed marmoset inhabits forests of central Brazil, Bolivia, and northwestern Paraguay and is named for its distinctive black tail. It occupies a variety of forest types in the southern Brazilian Amazon including gallery forests and cerradão. It is one of the more southerly Amazonian marmosets and shows greater tolerance for seasonal and semi-open habitats than its northern congeners.

children.name: Mico humeralifer
children.description: The Santarém marmoset has a distinctive pattern of white shoulders and rump contrasting with a dark body and reddish tail, making it one of the most visually striking of the Amazonian marmosets. It is restricted to forests between the Tapajós and Madeira rivers in Pará, Brazil. Like all Mico species it is a cooperative breeder dependent on intact Amazonian forest for its complex gum-feeding ecology.

children.name: Mico chrysoleucos
children.description: The gold-and-white marmoset is named for its striking golden-yellow and white pelage and is endemic to a small area of the Brazilian Amazon between the lower Juruena and Tapajós rivers. It was described by Spix in 1823 from a small collection area. As with many Mico marmosets, the boundaries of its range are delimited by major river barriers — a pattern driving the extraordinary diversity of this genus.

children.name: Mico nigriceps
children.description: The black-headed marmoset is restricted to the Brazilian Amazon between the Aripuanã and Juruena rivers, where it occupies terra firme and gallery forests. Its black crown contrasts sharply with white flanks and a pale face. It is threatened by the rapid deforestation of the MT-320 highway corridor running through its range in Mato Grosso.

children.name: Mico emiliae
children.description: Emilia's marmoset is a poorly known species of the eastern Brazilian Amazon, named in honour of Emilia Snethlage, a pioneering German ornithologist who worked at the Museu Paraense Emílio Goeldi. Its range lies between the Xingu and Tapajós rivers. It is considered Data Deficient by the IUCN due to limited field surveys.

children.name: Mico manicorensis
children.description: The Manicoré marmoset was described in 2000 from specimens collected near the town of Manicoré in Amazonas, Brazil. It is restricted to a small area between the Madeira and Aripuanã rivers. Like all Amazonian marmosets its range is likely delimited by river barriers and it is threatened by deforestation associated with the BR-319 highway reconstruction.

children.name: Mico marcai
children.description: Marca's marmoset was described in 1992 from a small area of Amazonas state between the Roosevelt and Aripuanã rivers, named for the field biologist Alfredo Marçal. It is one of the least-known marmosets, with only a handful of field observations. Its range is small and it is considered Vulnerable to the ongoing deforestation pressure that is increasingly reaching remote areas of southern Amazonia.

children.name: Mico intermedius
children.description: The Aripuanã marmoset inhabits a narrow interfluvial area between the Aripuanã and Roosevelt rivers in Mato Grosso and Amazonas, Brazil. It has a whitish or pale body with a contrasting dark-tipped tail. Its biology in the wild remains largely undescribed and it is evaluated as Data Deficient by the IUCN due to the lack of survey data from its remote range.

children.name: Mico leucippe
children.description: The white marmoset, almost entirely white or very pale throughout, is endemic to a small area of eastern Pará between the Tapajós and lower Amazon. It is one of the most visually distinctive Amazonian marmosets but poorly studied due to the remoteness of its range. Deforestation associated with agriculture in eastern Pará represents the primary threat to the species.

children.name: Mico acariensis
children.description: The Rio Acari marmoset was described in 2000 from specimens collected along the Acari River in Amazonas, Brazil. It has a whitish to buff body with an orange-red tail and dark head markings. Extremely limited data exist on its range, ecology, and population size, and like several other Mico species it was discovered during surveys of Amazonian interfluvial areas that had never previously been sampled by biologists.

children.name: Mico saterei
children.description: The Sateré marmoset was described in 2000 and is named after the Sateré-Mawé indigenous people who inhabit the same area between the Andirá and lower Juruá rivers in Amazonas. It is considered Vulnerable and has a very restricted range. It was discovered during biological surveys organised in the context of sustainable development projects with indigenous communities.

children.name: Mico oryzivora
children.description: The rice marmoset — named from an early, erroneous belief that it raided rice crops — is endemic to the Marajó Island and adjacent lower Amazon delta. It is unique among Mico in occupying an island and deltaic environment rather than upland Amazonian forest. It is considered Vulnerable due to the increasingly rapid clearance of vegetation on Marajó Island for cattle farming.

children.name: Mico roscidus
children.description: The wet marmoset is a distinctive species with dark grey-agouti dorsum and whitish underparts, known only from a restricted area of Rondônia and Mato Grosso in Brazil. Its common name reflects the grey-toned fur that Elliot Thomas coined as 'roscidus' (dewy). Deforestation in Rondônia has been severe and rapid, threatening this species across much of its limited range.

children.name: Mico rondoni
children.description: The Rondônia marmoset was described in 2011 from the state of Rondônia in Brazil, in the interfluvium between the Madeira and Ji-Paraná rivers. It is named for Rondônia state, itself named after the explorer Cândido Rondon. Deforestation rates in Rondônia are among the highest in the Brazilian Amazon, and the species is listed as Vulnerable.

children.name: Callimico
children.description: Goeldi's monkey is an anomalous callitrichid that combines marmoset-like morphology with tamarin-like features: it gives birth to single offspring (unlike the typical twin-births of marmosets) and retains third molars absent in other callitrichids. It is the sole member of its genus.

children.name: Callimico goeldii
children.description: Goeldi's monkey is a small, uniformly black callitrichid of the upper Amazon basin in Bolivia, Peru, Brazil, Ecuador, and Colombia. Named for Swiss-Brazilian naturalist Émil Goeldi, it inhabits dense bamboo stands and undergrowth and often associates with mixed-species troops of other callitrichids. Its single-birth reproductive strategy, retained molars, and molecular data place it as an early branch within the Callitrichidae, with an ancestral body plan.

children.name: Saguinus
children.description: The true tamarins — a diverse genus of small, long-clawed primates of Central and South American forests, often bearing elaborate facial ornamentation including mustaches, crests, and manes. Like marmosets, they routinely produce twins and live in cooperatively breeding family groups.

children.name: Saguinus oedipus
children.description: The cotton-top tamarin is one of the most endangered primates in the world, restricted to a small area of dry tropical forest in northwestern Colombia. Its spectacular white crest of long hair earned it the common name. It is Critically Endangered, with fewer than 6,000 individuals remaining, owing to habitat destruction and a history of mass exportation for biomedical research in the 1960s and 1970s.

children.name: Saguinus imperator
children.description: The emperor tamarin is named for a fancied resemblance of its sweeping white moustache to the beard of German Emperor Wilhelm II. It inhabits lowland tropical rainforest in southern Peru, northern Bolivia, and the southwestern Brazilian Amazon. It commonly forms mixed-species troops with saddleback tamarins, an association that provides greater vigilance against aerial and terrestrial predators.

children.name: Saguinus midas
children.description: The red-handed tamarin is named for its bright orange hands and feet that stand out against its otherwise black body. It ranges across Suriname, Guyana, French Guiana, and the adjacent Brazilian states of Amapá and Pará north of the Amazon. It is one of the few callitrichids that regularly inhabits secondary and degraded forest, making it less sensitive to forest fragmentation than most tamarins.

children.name: Saguinus fuscicollis
children.description: The saddleback tamarin is the most taxonomically complex tamarin, with up to 11 subspecies forming a species complex across the western and upper Amazon basin. Its common name refers to the contrasting 'saddle' pattern on the back formed by two-toned fur. Many subspecies may merit full species status, and recent molecular work is progressively elevating them, making this one of the most taxonomically dynamic mammal groups.

children.name: Saguinus mystax
children.description: The moustached tamarin inhabits the Peruvian and Bolivian Amazon and adjacent parts of Brazil, characterised by a prominent white moustache patch that contrasts with its dark face. It frequently associates with emperor tamarins in mixed-species groups. Long-term studies at the Cocha Cashu Biological Station in Manu National Park have illuminated its polyandrous social system in which multiple males cooperate to raise young.

children.name: Saguinus labiatus
children.description: The white-lipped tamarin inhabits the upper Amazon basin in Brazil, Bolivia, and Peru. Its most distinctive feature is a red chest contrasting with white lips. Like other tamarins it lives in cooperatively breeding groups of 3–8 in which all adult group members help carry and provision the infants born to the dominant female.

children.name: Saguinus nigricollis
children.description: The black-mantle tamarin inhabits the upper Amazon of Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Brazil, characterised by a black head, neck, and mantle contrasting with an orange-red rump and tail. It belongs to the nigricollis species group and often forms mixed-species associations with Saguinus fuscicollis subspecies in the same forests. It is Least Concern but faces pressure from forest clearance for agriculture.

children.name: Saguinus bicolor
children.description: The pied tamarin is Critically Endangered and uniquely adapted to urban forest in and around Manaus, the largest city in the Amazon basin. Its bare pinkish face is entirely unadorned and contrasts with a stark black-and-white body pattern. The expansion of Manaus across the only known range of this species makes it a flagship for urban primate conservation in the Amazon.

children.name: Saguinus leucopus
children.description: The white-footed tamarin is Endangered and endemic to a small area of inter-Andean forests in northern Colombia, between the Cauca and Magdalena rivers. It inhabits dry and humid forests in valley bottoms and lower slopes and is severely threatened by deforestation for agriculture. Colombia's fastest rates of forest clearance have pushed this little-studied tamarin into rapid decline.

children.name: Saguinus geoffroyi
children.description: Geoffroy's tamarin ranges from Panama into northwestern Colombia and is the only tamarin regularly found in Central America. Named for French naturalist Étienne Geoffroy Saint-Hilaire, it has a bald head, red nape, and mottled black-and-white body. It is one of the most studied callitrichids in Central America, with long-term research in Panama documenting its complex cooperative breeding behaviour.

children.name: Saguinus inustus
children.description: The mottle-faced tamarin is a large, plain tamarin of the northwest Amazon — Venezuela, Colombia, and Brazil — with uniformly dark brown fur and a mottled grey face. It inhabits terra firme and várzea forests north of the upper Amazon. It is among the least colourful of tamarins and has been little studied in the field, with most data coming from museum specimens.

children.name: Saguinus martinsi
children.description: Martins's tamarin is found between the lower Amazon, Trombetas, and Nhamundá rivers in Pará, Brazil, and was elevated to species status from a subspecies of Saguinus midas. It has predominantly black fur with orange-red hands and feet, similar to the midas group. Very limited field data exist on this species and its conservation status is assessed as Data Deficient.

children.name: Saguinus graellsi
children.description: The Río Napo tamarin inhabits the Napo region of Ecuador and Peru and was named for Spanish zoologist Mariano de la Paz Graells. It belongs to the nigricollis species group and has a black head and mantle with reddish-brown flanks. It is often treated as a subspecies of Saguinus nigricollis but molecular evidence increasingly supports its full species status.

children.name: Saguinus pileatus
children.description: The red-capped tamarin is endemic to the eastern Amazon of Brazil between the Madeira and Aripuanã rivers, identified by its reddish cap on an otherwise dark body. It was formally described by Pinto in 1931 and has only been observed in a handful of field studies. Like most Amazonian tamarins it appears to form mixed-species groups with sympatric marmosets.

children.name: Saguinus tripartitus
children.description: The golden-mantle tamarin of eastern Ecuador and adjacent Peru is a striking callitrichid with a golden-orange mantle contrasting with a black head and white ear tufts. It inhabits terra firme and flooded forests of the Napo basin. It is often placed as a subspecies of the moustached tamarin group but is generally regarded as a full species.

children.name: Saguinus illigeri
children.description: Illiger's saddle-back tamarin, named for German zoologist Johann Karl Wilhelm Illiger, occupies forests of central Peru east of the Andes. It was long treated as a subspecies of the saddleback tamarin complex but is now recognised as a distinct species based on pelage characters. Like other members of the fuscicollis complex it commonly forms mixed-species associations with larger tamarins.

children.name: Saguinus weddelli
children.description: Weddell's saddle-back tamarin, named for French-British botanist Hugh Algernon Weddell, inhabits forests of Bolivia, Peru, and adjacent Brazil. It belongs to the fuscicollis species group, bearing the characteristic two-toned 'saddle' pattern on its back. It is Least Concern and is one of the more commonly encountered tamarins in the southern Amazon basin.

children.name: Saguinus melanoleucus
children.description: The white-mantled tamarin is restricted to a small area of the Brazilian state of Amazonas between the Juruá and Purús rivers, bearing a striking white or pale mantle on a dark body. It is classified as Data Deficient due to the very limited survey data from its remote range. It is part of the fuscicollis species group and is considered one of the most recently evolved members of the complex.

children.name: Saguinus ursulus
children.description: The bear-face tamarin inhabits forests south of the Amazon in Pará, Brazil. It was split from Saguinus midas based on distribution and subtle pelage differences. Its large dark eyes in a black face give the slightly bear-like appearance suggested by its name. Very little field data exist on the ecology of this species in its range.

children.name: Saguinus niger
children.description: The black tamarin is one of the midas group, uniformly black-bodied with orange-red extremities, inhabiting forests in the lower Amazon and Tocantins river regions of Pará, Brazil. Like the closely related pied tamarin it is threatened by deforestation in one of the most cleared parts of the Brazilian Amazon. It has been the subject of limited field studies due to the difficult accessibility of its primary habitat.

children.name: Leontopithecus
children.description: Lion tamarins are among the most endangered primates in the world — brilliantly coloured small monkeys with a mane of long hair framing the face, restricted to the Atlantic Forest fragments of southeastern Brazil. Intensive conservation breeding programmes have significantly improved their numbers.

children.name: Leontopithecus rosalia
children.description: The golden lion tamarin is the flagship species of Atlantic Forest conservation — its brilliant golden-orange fur and lion-like mane making it one of the most recognisable primates in the world. Critically Endangered, it nearly went extinct in the 1970s before an international breeding and reintroduction programme raised its wild population to over 3,700 individuals by the 2020s. It is restricted to lowland Atlantic Forest in Rio de Janeiro state.

children.name: Leontopithecus chrysomelas
children.description: The golden-headed lion tamarin is Endangered and restricted to the lowland and foothill Atlantic Forest of southern Bahia and northern Minas Gerais, Brazil. Its black body, golden head, and golden-orange limbs give it a striking tricolour appearance. The cocoa agroforestry ('cabruca') system of southern Bahia has historically provided habitat but is being replaced by more intensive cattle ranching.

children.name: Leontopithecus chrysopygus
children.description: The black lion tamarin is Critically Endangered, restricted to only a few forest patches in São Paulo state — primarily the Morro do Diabo State Park — with a total population of under 1,500 individuals. Once widespread in the interior Atlantic Forest of São Paulo, 90% of its habitat has been destroyed for sugarcane and cattle ranching. Its golden rump and thighs contrast with its otherwise black body.

children.name: Leontopithecus caissara
children.description: The black-faced lion tamarin was discovered only in 1990 on Superagüi Island and adjacent mainland in Paraná, Brazil, making it one of the last large mammals described from South America in the twentieth century. It is Critically Endangered with fewer than 400 individuals, restricted to coastal Atlantic Forest in and around the Guaraqueçaba Environmental Protection Area. Its distinctive black face and forequarters contrast with a golden-yellow body.

children.name: Aotus
children.description: Night monkeys, or owl monkeys, are the only truly nocturnal anthropoid primates — a unique adaptation among higher primates. They have enormous, forward-facing eyes adapted for low-light vision and live in small, strictly monogamous family groups in which the father carries infants.

children.name: Aotus trivirgatus
children.description: The three-striped night monkey is the northernmost owl monkey, inhabiting forests of Venezuela, the Guianas, and northern Brazil. It is named for the three dark facial stripes that converge on the forehead — a pattern shared by all Aotus. As the type species of the genus it has been widely used in biomedical research, particularly in malaria vaccine research, due to its susceptibility to human malaria parasites.

children.name: Aotus azarae
children.description: Azara's night monkey, named for Spanish naturalist Félix de Azara, is the most southerly owl monkey, ranging across Bolivia, Paraguay, northern Argentina, and southwestern Brazil. Unlike most Aotus it is active at twilight (cathemeral) and sometimes forages by day. Long-term studies in the Argentinian Chaco have shown extreme male investment in offspring care — fathers carry infants more than 90% of the time.

children.name: Aotus vociferans
children.description: Spix's night monkey inhabits the western Amazon basin in Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, and adjacent Brazil, and produces remarkably loud owl-like hoots that carry through the forest at night — giving the species its name vociferans (loud-voiced). It belongs to the grey-necked group of Aotus. Field studies have shown strict monogamy and biparental care with the male carrying infants for up to 95% of the time.

children.name: Aotus nancymaae
children.description: Nancy Ma's night monkey was named for Nancy Ma, a primatologist at the San Antonio Zoo who maintained a captive research colony. It is found in Peru south of the Amazon and east of the Ucayali. This species has been extensively used in malaria research as it readily supports infection by Plasmodium falciparum, contributing to the development of candidate malaria vaccines.

children.name: Aotus nigriceps
children.description: The black-headed night monkey inhabits the forests of Bolivia, Peru, and southwestern Brazil, belonging to the red-necked group of Aotus distinguished by reddish-orange rather than grey neck colouration. It is one of the larger owl monkeys and is Least Concern. Like all Aotus it forms permanent pair bonds and the male provides most parental care after the first weeks of the infant's life.

children.name: Aotus lemurinus
children.description: The lemurine night monkey is found in the Andean foothills and lowland forests of Colombia, Ecuador, Panama, and Venezuela, and is one of the most threatened owl monkeys due to the severe deforestation of Andean foothill forests. It belongs to the grey-necked group and was one of the first species described in the genus. It is listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN.

children.name: Aotus miconax
children.description: The Peruvian night monkey is restricted to the cloud forests of the Andes in Peru from around 1,000 to 2,500 m elevation and is listed as Vulnerable. It belongs to the grey-necked group and inhabits high-altitude forests that have been extensively cleared for agriculture. Very little is known of its natural history in the wild. It is occasionally encountered in the vicinity of indigenous communities in the eastern Andes.

children.name: Aotus brumbacki
children.description: Brumback's night monkey, named for American neurologist Robert Brumback, is known only from the eastern lowlands of Colombia in the Orinoco and Meta river drainages. It is a grey-necked species and is listed as Vulnerable, threatened by the extensive conversion of Colombian eastern lowland forests and savannahs for agriculture and cattle ranching. It was described in 1983 based on a small series of specimens and field observations.

children.name: Aotus griseimembra
children.description: The grey-legged night monkey inhabits forests of northwestern Colombia and northwestern Venezuela and is one of the most northern members of the grey-necked Aotus group. It is Endangered due to the rapid loss of lowland forests in its range. It was previously treated as a subspecies of Aotus lemurinus and was elevated to full species based on chromosomal and molecular data.

children.name: Aotus zonalis
children.description: The Panamanian night monkey is found in forests from Panama through northwestern Colombia and is the most northerly representative of the genus alongside Aotus lemurinus. It was long treated as a subspecies but genetic data support its recognition as a distinct species. It inhabits both dry and wet lowland forests and is listed as Vulnerable due to forest clearance in its range.

children.name: Callicebus
children.description: Titi monkeys of the Callicebus group are small, shy primates of Amazonian forests, famous for their spectacular dawn duets between mated pairs and their habit of tail-twining — sitting side by side with tails entwined as a social bonding behaviour.

children.name: Callicebus torquatus
children.description: The collared titi is a widespread north-Amazonian titi monkey ranging from Venezuela and Colombia through the Guianas and into northern Brazil. Its rufous or orange collar against a dark body makes it one of the more recognisable titi monkeys. It is strictly frugivorous and uses dawn duets to reinforce pair bonds and advertise territory to neighbouring groups.

children.name: Callicebus lugens
children.description: The widow titi is a dark, melanic titi monkey of the northwest Amazon, occurring in Colombia, Venezuela, and adjacent Brazil, in flooded and terra firme forests with blackwater rivers. Its sombre black-and-brown pelage distinguishes it from the more colourful relatives, and it inhabits areas associated with white-sand and blackwater ecosystems. It is Least Concern and relatively well represented in protected areas.

children.name: Plecturocebus
children.description: Plecturocebus is one of three genera into which titi monkeys were recently divided. These are small, monogamous, frugivorous primates of Amazonian forests characterised by soft thick fur and the habit of duetting at dawn with their lifelong mates.

children.name: Plecturocebus moloch
children.description: The dusky titi is a broadly distributed Amazonian titi monkey of central and eastern Brazil, occupying forests between the Tapajós and Tocantins rivers south of the Amazon. It was the type species for the original broad Callicebus genus before the group was split. It feeds predominantly on fruit supplemented by insects and leaves and lives in small family groups of 2–5.

children.name: Plecturocebus donacophilus
children.description: The white-eared titi monkey inhabits forests of Bolivia and adjacent Brazil and Peru in the southwestern Amazon basin. It is characterised by white-tipped ears against a reddish-brown body. It is Least Concern and its range includes several national parks in Bolivia, although deforestation of forests in the Beni and Santa Cruz departments is reducing suitable habitat.

children.name: Plecturocebus cupreus
children.description: The coppery titi inhabits forests of the upper Amazon in Peru and adjacent Brazil, named for its warm copper-red ventral colouration. Long-term studies of this species and closely related forms at Cocha Cashu in Manu National Park established the framework for titi monkey socioecology: strict monogamy, biparental care, and strong intersexual bonds. It is Least Concern within its protected range.

children.name: Plecturocebus caligatus
children.description: The chestnut-bellied titi is found in the central Brazilian Amazon between the Juruá and Purús rivers, with a pale whitish face contrasting with chestnut underparts. It is listed as Least Concern but the forests of this region face increasing pressure from road construction and agricultural expansion. It is a typical titi monkey in its monogamous social system and dawn-duetting behaviour.

children.name: Plecturocebus aureipalatii
children.description: The Golden Palace titi has the distinction of being the only primate named after an internet casino: the online casino GoldenPalace.com paid $650,000 at a 2004 charity auction to name the newly discovered species. It was described from forests in the Bolivian Amazon near Río Madre de Dios. Despite its fanciful name it is a real, valid species and is listed as Least Concern.

children.name: Plecturocebus caquetensis
children.description: The Caquetá titi was described only in 2010 from the Caquetá department of Colombia — one of the most conflict-affected areas of the country — where it inhabits highly fragmented forests. It is Critically Endangered with fewer than 250 individuals estimated; its range has been severely reduced by deforestation and armed conflict that historically impeded scientific surveys. Conservation of remaining fragments is critical for its survival.

children.name: Plecturocebus lucifer
children.description: The Lucifer titi inhabits forests of the western Amazon in Peru, Ecuador, and Colombia and is part of the torquatus group, with rich reddish-brown underparts. It is listed as Least Concern and occurs in several protected areas including the Yasuni and Cuyabeno reserves in Ecuador. Its population status is not well quantified but it appears stable in undisturbed forest.

children.name: Pithecia
children.description: Sakis are medium-sized pitheciid primates of Amazonian forests, characterised by dense, rough coats and bushy tails used for balance. They are highly specialised seed predators, using their procumbent incisors and strong jaw muscles to crack hard-coated seeds that other primates cannot open.

children.name: Pithecia pithecia
children.description: The white-faced saki is the best-known saki monkey, with adult males bearing a dramatically pale white or golden face contrasting with a black body, while females and young are brown-agouti. It is found in Venezuela, Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana, and northern Brazil. It is a canopy species, leaping between trees with surprising agility for a medium-sized primate.

children.name: Pithecia monachus
children.description: The monk saki ranges across the western Amazon basin in Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Brazil, where it inhabits lowland terra firme and flooded forests. Its common name derives from the fringe of long hair surrounding the face like a monk's cowl. It is Least Concern but the 2019 Rylands revision of the genus elevated several former subspecies to full species status.

children.name: Pithecia irrorata
children.description: The Rio Tapajós saki, also called the bald-faced saki, inhabits forests between the Tapajós and Madeira rivers in the central Brazilian Amazon. The adult male has a nearly white face with a grizzled pepper-and-salt dorsum. It is Least Concern but populations face pressure from deforestation and the bushmeat trade in Para state.

children.name: Pithecia albicans
children.description: The white saki is endemic to the forests between the Juruá and upper Purús rivers in Amazonas, Brazil. Males are largely white to cream-coloured, an exceptionally pale saki, while females are more brownish-grey. It is listed as Vulnerable due to the deforestation of its restricted range. It was first described by Spix from specimens obtained along the lower Juruá.

children.name: Pithecia aequatorialis
children.description: The equatorial saki is found in forests of Ecuador, Peru, and Colombia in the Napo and Pastaza river drainages. It was elevated from subspecies status following the comprehensive 2019 revision of the genus. Males have a distinctive pale forehead and face with dark body; females are brown-grey. It inhabits high-canopy undisturbed forest and is sensitive to hunting.

children.name: Pithecia hirsuta
children.description: The hairy saki inhabits forests of the Ucayali basin in Peru, distinguished by its extremely shaggy coat. It was elevated to full species status in the 2019 Rylands et al. revision and represents the westernmost member of the monachus group. As with other sakis, the male's facial colouration is a key identification feature and differs markedly between sexes.

children.name: Pithecia chrysocephala
children.description: The golden-headed saki inhabits forests north of the Amazon and west of the Rio Negro in Venezuela, Colombia, and Brazil, bearing a striking golden-orange crown that distinguishes it from all other sakis. It was described originally as a subspecies but recent revisions recognise it as a full species. It is Least Concern and relatively well-protected in the Serra da Neblina and Pico da Neblina national parks.

children.name: Pithecia vanzolinii
children.description: Vanzolini's saki is named for Paulo Vanzolini, the Brazilian herpetologist and samba composer who made major contributions to Amazonian biogeography. It inhabits forests in the interfluvium between the Purús and Juruá rivers. Its taxonomy was clarified in the 2019 genus revision and it is now considered a valid species distinct from sympatric saki forms.

children.name: Chiropotes
children.description: Bearded sakis are specialised seed predators of Amazonian and Guiana Shield forests, distinguished by bilobed parietal swellings on the head and prominent beards. They are highly mobile and travel large distances daily in large groups to exploit widely dispersed hard-seeded fruit resources.

children.name: Chiropotes satanas
children.description: The black bearded saki is Critically Endangered, restricted to the eastern Brazilian Amazon in Pará, where it is seriously threatened by reservoir flooding for the Tucuruí and Belo Monte hydroelectric dams, habitat loss, and hunting. Its all-black pelage makes it one of the most striking Amazonian primates. It lives in large groups of 20–40 that travel widely through the canopy to locate the hard seeds and unripe fruits that are its dietary specialty.

children.name: Chiropotes albinasus
children.description: The white-nosed bearded saki is Vulnerable, characterised by a sharply contrasting white nose and muzzle against a dark body. It inhabits forests south of the Amazon between the Tapajós and Xingu rivers in Brazil, a region experiencing some of the highest deforestation rates in Amazonia. It lives in large, cohesive groups and is particularly sensitive to forest fragmentation due to its wide daily ranging.

children.name: Chiropotes chiropotes
children.description: The Guiana bearded saki is found across the Guiana Shield in Venezuela, Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana, and adjacent northern Brazil. It is characterised by reddish-brown to chestnut back colour with a black cap and beard. It is Least Concern and relatively well protected in the large national parks of the Guiana Shield. Its bilobed cranial swellings are a remarkable anatomical specialisation unique among primates.

children.name: Chiropotes israelita
children.description: The brown-backed bearded saki inhabits forests of the upper Rio Negro and adjacent Colombia and Venezuela, a biodiverse region of northwestern Amazonia characterised by blackwater rivers and white-sand forests. It was elevated to full species status from the chiropotes group based on molecular and morphological data. It is listed as Least Concern and inhabits areas that remain relatively undisturbed.

children.name: Chiropotes utahickae
children.description: Uta Hick's bearded saki is named for German zoo director Uta Hick and inhabits forests between the Xingu and Tocantins rivers in eastern Pará, Brazil. It was described in 2007 as a new species. Its range overlaps with the Belo Monte dam reservoir and deforestation for soy and cattle in the region makes it one of the more threatened bearded sakis.

children.name: Cacajao
children.description: Uakaris are highly distinctive pitheciid primates with bald, vividly coloured red faces and unusually short tails for New World monkeys. They are specialised for flooded and seasonally inundated forests of the western Amazon and are among the most range-restricted of all primates.

children.name: Cacajao calvus
children.description: The bald uakari is the emblematic primate of the western Amazon's várzea floodplain forests, with its shocking scarlet-red bald face standing out even at distance in the canopy. Facial redness correlates with good health and parasite load, functioning as an honest signal in mate choice. It inhabits flooded forests of the Juruá and Purús river systems in Brazil and Peru and is listed as Vulnerable.

children.name: Cacajao melanocephalus
children.description: The black-headed uakari is found north of the Amazon in the Rio Negro and Branco drainages, Colombia, and Venezuela, distinguished from the bald uakari by its black head and golden to reddish-brown body. It inhabits igapó (blackwater flooded) forests and is highly specialised for seeds and unripe fruit. Its short stumpy tail makes it unique among tailed New World monkeys.

children.name: Cacajao ayresi
children.description: Ayres' black uakari was described in 2008 from the Japurá-Solimões basin in Brazil and named after the late conservation biologist José Márcio Ayres, who pioneered uakari research at Mamirauá. It has a predominantly black face and very dark body, distinguishing it from adjacent populations of other uakaris. Very little is known of its ecology but it is presumed to occupy várzea and igapó forests like its congeners.

children.name: Cacajao hosomi
children.description: The Neblina uakari was described in 2008 from the tepui foothills near the Venezuela-Brazil border in the upper Río Negro region — one of the most remote and least-visited areas of the Amazon. It is the most recently described uakari and inhabits the cloud forest edges of the Pico da Neblina massif. Very little field data exist and its conservation status has not been formally assessed.
